walkthrough.title: Find a Stable Surface
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Jenga. First, find a flat, sturdy table. Avoid wobbly or uneven surfaces. A bare table works best because tablecloths can shift and cause the tower to fall prematurely. Make sure everyone can comfortably reach the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Use the Loading Tray
walkthrough.text: Grab the plastic loading tray and stand it upright on the table. This sleeve helps you build a perfectly straight tower. Load blocks in groups of three side by side, alternating direction ninety degrees with each level. Keep stacking until all fifty-four blocks are used, which makes eighteen levels.

walkthrough.title: Remove the Tray
walkthrough.text: Carefully slide the loading tray up and away from the tower, leaving it freestanding. Gently adjust any blocks that are sticking out to make the tower as uniform and square as possible. The neater the tower starts, the longer the game lasts.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Player
walkthrough.text: The person who built the tower goes first, or pick someone at random. Play goes clockwise from there. Everyone should be seated or standing where they can reach the tower from their side. Ready to pull? Let's learn the rules.

summary.title: Find a Stable Surface
bullets: Use a flat, sturdy table — no wobbles, no tablecloth
All players need comfortable reach to the tower

summary.title: Use the Loading Tray
bullets: Stand the loading tray upright on the table
Load 3 blocks per level, alternating 90° each level
54 blocks = 18 levels

summary.title: Remove the Tray
bullets: Slide tray away carefully → tower is freestanding
Square up any protruding blocks

summary.title: Choose the First Player
bullets: Tower builder goes first (or random)
Play proceeds clockwise

walkthrough.title: The Goal
walkthrough.text: Jenga is simple. On your turn, remove one block from the tower and place it on top without knocking the tower over. The player who causes the tower to collapse loses. Everyone else wins. That is the entire game, but the execution is where all the tension lives.

walkthrough.title: One Hand Only
walkthrough.text: You may only use one hand at a time to touch the tower. You can switch which hand you use during your turn, but only one hand may contact the tower at any moment. No bracing with your other hand, your body, or any object. This is the fundamental rule that makes Jenga challenging.

walkthrough.title: Choose and Test a Block
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you can gently tap or push blocks to test which ones are loose. You may test as many blocks as you like. If a block does not move easily, leave it alone and try a different one. You can only remove blocks from below the highest completed level of the tower.

walkthrough.title: Remove the Block
walkthrough.text: Once you find a loose block, carefully slide it out with one hand. If you start pulling a block but change your mind, you must either fully remove it or push it back to its original position using one hand. Take your time. There is no official time limit.

walkthrough.title: Place on Top
walkthrough.text: Place the removed block on the topmost level of the tower. The new level must be perpendicular to the level below it, following the alternating pattern. Each level holds exactly three blocks side by side. When a level has three blocks, the next block starts a new level.

walkthrough.title: Turn Completion
walkthrough.text: Your turn does not end until the tower is stable and you have removed your hand. If the tower is wobbling, wait for it to settle. Once it is still, play passes to the next player clockwise. The next player inherits whatever wobbly, leaning mess you left behind.

walkthrough.title: Tower Collapse
walkthrough.text: If the tower falls during your turn, you lose. The collapse can happen while you are removing a block, placing it on top, or even while the tower is settling after your move. If just one block falls but the tower stays mostly standing, your group decides if that counts as a collapse. Generally, if more than one extra block falls, the tower has collapsed.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Replaying
walkthrough.text: There is no point system. You either caused the collapse or you did not. The last player who successfully completed a turn is the winner. To play again, simply rebuild the tower using the loading tray and start a new game. Many groups track losses across multiple games for a session champion.

summary.title: The Goal
bullets: Remove 1 block, place it on top, don't collapse the tower
Player who causes collapse loses; everyone else wins

summary.title: One Hand Only
bullets: Only 1 hand may touch the tower at a time
May switch hands, but never both simultaneously
No bracing with other hand, body, or objects

summary.title: Choose and Test a Block
bullets: Tap/push blocks to test looseness — test as many as you like
Only remove from below the highest completed level

summary.title: Remove the Block
bullets: Slide the chosen block out with one hand
Changed your mind? Must fully remove or push back to original position

summary.title: Place on Top
bullets: Place block on topmost level, perpendicular to level below
3 blocks per level → then start a new level

summary.title: Turn Completion
bullets: Turn ends when tower is stable and hand is removed
Wait for wobbling to settle before releasing

summary.title: Tower Collapse
bullets: Tower falls = you lose (during removal, placement, or settling)
1 block falls but tower stands = group decides if it counts
Multiple blocks falling = collapse

summary.title: Winning and Replaying
bullets: No points — binary win/lose
Last successful player is the winner
Rebuild with loading tray for another game

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Tower
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Jenga! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a few rounds with four players. You will see how to test blocks, make smart choices, and avoid the most common mistakes that bring towers crashing down. Let's start pulling.

walkthrough.title: The Fresh Tower
walkthrough.text: The tower is built and standing tall at eighteen levels. It looks perfectly neat and symmetrical right now. Every block is snug. The trick is finding blocks that are slightly looser than others, because manufacturing differences mean not all blocks fit the same way.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Testing Blocks
walkthrough.text: Player 1 goes first. They gently push on the center block of a low level. It slides easily because center blocks have two neighbors supporting the level. Player 1 pushes it all the way through from one side to the other and catches it on the opposite side. Then they place it on top of the tower, perpendicular to the level below. Clean first move.

walkthrough.title: Why Center Blocks First
walkthrough.text: Center blocks are usually the easiest to remove in the early game. You can push them straight through from one side. The two edge blocks stay in place and continue supporting the tower. Removing a center block also creates two loose edge blocks that other players can use later.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Edge Block
walkthrough.text: Player 2 decides to pull an edge block. They gently tap it from the inside and it slides outward. They carefully extract it and place it on top. The level now has only one block left, which is stable because the weight above is centered. Good move by Player 2.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Testing Multiple Blocks
walkthrough.text: Player 3 taps several blocks before committing. The first one does not budge. The second feels tight. The third slides a tiny bit. That is the one. Player 3 takes their time sliding it out slowly and places it on top. Remember, there is no time limit, so patience is your best friend.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Rushing
walkthrough.text: Player 4 grabs a block quickly without testing it first. It is tight and resists. Player 4 yanks harder, and the tower wobbles dangerously. The tower survives, but it is now leaning noticeably. Player 4 got lucky. Rushing is the number one cause of early collapses.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Test Before You Pull
walkthrough.text: Always test multiple blocks before committing. A gentle push or tap tells you everything you need to know. If a block does not move easily, leave it alone. There are fifty-four blocks in the tower and plenty of loose ones to choose from. Never force a block that is bearing weight.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Two Hands
walkthrough.text: A few rounds later, Player 4 is pulling a tricky block. The tower starts to wobble and instinctively, Player 4 reaches with their other hand to steady it. That is a rule violation. You may only use one hand at a time. If other players catch you, the move does not count and you must try again.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: One Hand, Every Time
walkthrough.text: The one-hand rule is the soul of Jenga. It forces you to be delicate and precise. Train yourself to keep your free hand behind your back or at your side. If the tower wobbles after your move, let it settle on its own. Your second hand will only make things worse.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Bad Placement
walkthrough.text: Player 3 removes a block and places it off-center on top, creating a lean. Over the next few turns, other players add blocks to the same side. The lean gets worse with every turn. The tower is now dramatically tilted. Player 3's poor placement started a chain reaction that made every subsequent turn harder.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Place Blocks Carefully on Top
walkthrough.text: Where you place the block on top matters as much as which block you remove. Place blocks in the center of the level when possible to maintain balance. If the tower is leaning, place your block on the opposite side to counterbalance. A well-placed block can actually stabilize a wobbly tower.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations, you have survived your first game of Jenga! Remember the three key lessons: always test before pulling, use only one hand, and place blocks carefully on top. The tower gets more exciting with every turn. Now go play for real.

walkthrough.title: Before You Stack
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for techniques on reading the tower and choosing the right block.

Second, reference the Appendix for common house rules and block removal techniques.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Stack
bullets: Check out General Tips → reading the tower and block selection
Reference the Appendix → house rules and removal techniques
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the most common mistakes new Jenga players make. First, rushing through your turn instead of taking time to test blocks. Second, using two hands out of instinct when the tower wobbles. Third, placing blocks off-center on top, which creates a cumulative lean. Fourth, committing to a stuck block instead of backing off and trying another. Keep these in mind and your tower will last longer than everyone else's patience.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Tower
walkthrough.text: Before you touch anything, study the tower. Look for levels where one or two blocks have already been removed. Those levels have loose blocks. Check the tower's overall lean direction. If it is leaning left, removing a block from the right side is risky. Look for blocks that are slightly misaligned or sticking out. These visual cues tell you which blocks will come out easily.

walkthrough.title: Push Through, Don't Pull
walkthrough.text: Whenever possible, push center blocks through from one side rather than pulling them out. Pushing creates a smoother motion that rocks the tower less. Pulling requires gripping the block, which applies uneven force. Save pulling for edge blocks where pushing is not an option. Even with edge blocks, a gentle outward tap followed by a slow slide is better than a hard pull.

walkthrough.title: Placement Strategy
walkthrough.text: Where you place your block on top is just as important as which block you remove. Always try to place blocks in the center of the top level to maintain even weight distribution. If the tower is leaning, place your block on the side opposite the lean to counterbalance. A well-placed block can actually make the tower more stable than before your turn.

walkthrough.title: Never Commit to a Bad Block
walkthrough.text: If you start nudging a block and it feels tight, stop immediately. You are allowed to push it back to its original position and try a different one. The worst thing you can do is keep forcing a block that is clearly bearing weight. You have the entire tower below the top level to choose from. There is always a better option.

walkthrough.title: The Psychological Game
walkthrough.text: Jenga has a strong psychological element. When the tower is tall and wobbly, nerves take over. Take a deep breath before your turn. Steady your hand. Block out distractions from other players trying to make you laugh or flinch. The calmer you are, the smoother your movements. Rushing because you are nervous is the fastest way to lose.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't rush — take time to test blocks
Don't use two hands — keep free hand at your side
Don't place blocks off-center — creates cumulative lean
Don't force stuck blocks — try a different one

summary.title: Reading the Tower
bullets: Look for levels with 1-2 blocks already removed (loose options)
Check the tower's lean direction before choosing a side
Misaligned or protruding blocks = easier to extract

summary.title: Push Through, Don't Pull
bullets: Push center blocks through for smoother motion
Pulling applies uneven force → more wobble
Edge blocks: gentle outward tap, then slow slide

summary.title: Placement Strategy
bullets: Center placement on top = even weight distribution
If tower leans, place on opposite side to counterbalance
Good placement can stabilize the tower

summary.title: Never Commit to a Bad Block
bullets: Block feels tight? Stop and push it back
You can always try a different block
Never force a weight-bearing block

summary.title: The Psychological Game
bullets: Deep breath before your turn, steady your hand
Block out distractions from other players
Calm = smooth movements; nervous rushing = collapse

walkthrough.title: Strategic Block Removal
walkthrough.text: In competitive play, think about making the next player's turn harder. Remove a block that leaves the tower in a tricky state for your opponent. If you can remove a block that makes the tower lean slightly toward your opponent's side, they will have a harder time reaching around to find good blocks. Every block you remove changes the tower's weight distribution.

walkthrough.title: Weight Distribution Awareness
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think about the tower as a weight system. The top of the tower grows heavier as blocks accumulate. Lower levels bear more weight. Middle levels are often the sweet spot for removal because they are under moderate pressure. Avoid removing blocks from levels that are clearly supporting a heavy lean above them.

walkthrough.title: The Lean Game
walkthrough.text: Every tower develops a lean as blocks are removed. Advanced players track the lean direction and use it to their advantage. If the tower leans slightly left, remove blocks from the left side where weight has shifted away. These blocks are under less pressure and slide out more easily. Then place your block on the right side to push the lean back toward neutral.

walkthrough.title: Edge Versus Center Timing
walkthrough.text: In the early game, center blocks are easiest to remove. In the mid-game, many center blocks are already gone, so edge blocks become the primary targets. In the late game, full levels with all three blocks become rare and precious. Know which type of block to target based on the game phase. Late-game decisions often come down to which single remaining block in a level can be removed without collapsing everything.

walkthrough.title: Two-Block Level Strategy
walkthrough.text: When a level has only two blocks left, those blocks can often be separated for removal. Gently push one block sideways to create a gap. If the weight above is distributed evenly, either block can support the tower alone. This doubles your options on future turns. But be careful: if the weight above is off-center, removing the wrong block will bring everything down.

walkthrough.title: One-Block Level Tactics
walkthrough.text: A level with only one block remaining is a load-bearing pillar. Never try to remove it unless it is clearly not supporting anything above. In the late game, many levels have one block, creating a spine of single blocks stacked in an alternating zigzag. This is when Jenga gets most exciting. Each move becomes a life-or-death decision for the tower.

walkthrough.title: Speed Versus Precision
walkthrough.text: Some experienced players develop a fast technique: quickly flick a block out before the tower has time to react. This works for very loose blocks but is risky for anything else. The precision approach, slowly sliding a block millimeter by millimeter, is safer for tight blocks. Know when to use each technique based on how loose the block feels during testing.

walkthrough.title: Physical Technique
walkthrough.text: Your body position matters. Stand or sit so that your pulling arm is at a comfortable height relative to the tower. Use your fingertips, not your whole hand, for maximum control. Rest your elbow on the table if you need stability. Breathe steadily and make your moves on an exhale, when your body is most relaxed and your hands are steadiest.

summary.title: Strategic Block Removal
bullets: In competitive play, leave the tower tricky for the next player
Remove blocks that shift weight toward opponent's side
Every removal changes the weight distribution

summary.title: Weight Distribution Awareness
bullets: Lower levels bear more weight — approach carefully
Middle levels are the sweet spot for removal
Avoid levels supporting heavy lean above

summary.title: The Lean Game
bullets: Track the lean direction throughout the game
Remove from the lean side (less pressure on those blocks)
Place on opposite side to counterbalance

summary.title: Edge Versus Center Timing
bullets: Early game: center blocks easiest

entries.definition: 54 hardwood blocks, each approximately 1.5 cm by 2.5 cm by 7.5 cm. Slight manufacturing variations create different friction levels between blocks.
A plastic sleeve used to build the initial tower. Blocks are stacked inside it, then the tray is slid away to leave the tower freestanding.
Each horizontal layer of the tower contains exactly 3 blocks placed side by side. Each level is perpendicular to the one below it.
Only one hand may touch the tower at any time. You may switch hands during your turn, but only one hand can make contact at any given moment.
Gently tapping or pushing blocks to check looseness before committing to removal. You may test as many blocks as you like on your turn.
A removal technique where you push a center block from one side so it slides out the other. Creates smoother motion with less wobble than pulling.
A removal technique for edge blocks where you grip and slide the block outward. Requires more care to avoid tipping the tower.
When the tower falls or multiple blocks drop. The player whose action caused the collapse loses the game. All other players win.
Placing a block on the opposite side of the tower's lean to push the center of gravity back toward the middle. A key advanced technique.
The middle block in a three-block level. Often the easiest to remove by pushing through, especially in the early game.
One of the two outer blocks in a three-block level. Must be pulled outward rather than pushed through.
A common unofficial rule where each player has 30 or 60 seconds to complete their turn. Adds pressure and speeds up the game.
An unofficial rule where you must commit to the first block you touch. Dramatically increases difficulty and tension.

entries.term: House Rule: Two Hands for Kids
entries.definition: An unofficial rule allowing younger players to use both hands. Makes the game accessible for children while keeping the core experience.
